p01s02: In the course of time, a great quarrel arose between the Menon and the Namboothiris who were the temple's proprietors.
p01s03: Because of this, the Menon burned and destroyed the Devaswom's accounts, record-books and everything else.
p01s04: For that reason there now exists no documentary evidence at all for knowing the temple's old history.
p01s05: So this is being written relying only on the hearsay received from aged elders familiar with the old days.
p02s01: At the Ammathiruvadi temple the hereditary office-holder's account is still with the Vazhappilly Menon's family: it is still the custom that whoever is eldest in that family walks before the procession carrying a bundle of palm-leaf accounts.
p02s02: For that service, certain perquisites from the Devaswom are still given to them.
p02s03: In earlier times everyone used to call the eldest Menon of that family the "Kaakkunna Menon" — the Guarding Menon.
p02s04: That has worn down to "Kaakkara Menon".
p03s01: The Ammathiruvadi's true sacred name is "Thiruvalayannur Bhagavathi".
p03s02: In the Bhasha-Naishadha Champu of the famous "Mazhamangalathu Namboothiri",
p04s01: "O you whose tender creeper-body is woven of the fifty-one letters! O honey-nectar borne in the cluster of blossoms that flower lovingly on the branch of the tree that is the Veda!
p04s02: O treasure of beauty and fortune, won by the merit of humbling the pride of the flower-arrowed god! O Sovereign of Valaya, Mistress of the universe — I bow at your twin feet!"
p05s01: That opening verse is about this very Devi.
p05s02: It is well known that Mazhamangalathu Namboothiri's illam was at Urakam.
p05s03: The reason this Bhagavathi came to have this sacred name will become clear from the matters related below.
p06s01: The temple in question stands about seven nazhikas south of Thrissivaperur, within Perumanam — one of the sixty-four Brahmin villages of Kerala.
p06s02: Since the Brahmins whom Sri Parasurama first brought to Kerala went back, he — so that the second group would not likewise leave — moved their hair-tuft from the back of the head to the front, changed most of their customs, gave them the name Namboothiris, and settled them permanently in Kerala; and after he vanished from this world at Thrissivaperur in central Kerala, all the Namboothiris together resolved that, for the full prosperity of Kerala, they should go to Kanchipuram, worship Kamakshi Amman until she showed herself, bring her back, and enshrine her in Kerala; and for that purpose they chose and sent the "Thiruvalayannur" Bhattathiri, with the "Keezhottukara" Namboothiripad and the "Komarathu" Menon to assist him.
p06s03: All three went to Kanchipuram and worshipped with devotion; the Amman appeared and asked, "What is your desire?" — "Amman must come with us to Kerala," they humbly begged, and the Devi consented.
p07s01: The three returned to their homeland rejoicing.
p07s02: Reaching home, each set down the palm-leaf umbrella he had carried, went off to bathe and so on — and when they came back and tried to pick the umbrellas up, they could not be lifted.
p07s03: When an astrological inquiry was made into the cause, it was seen that the umbrellas would not move because the Devi had taken residence in them.
p07s04: So the Devi was worshipped at those very spots.
p08s01: After some time had passed thus, one day the Thiruvalayannur Bhattathiri had a dream.
p08s02: The dream was that someone came to him and said: he should make the western wing of his illam the sanctum and build a temple, and settle the Devi there; he should relinquish all the illam's property to the Devi and entrust the management of the Devaswom's affairs to the Kochi Raja; having done all this, the Bhattathiri should go north; a day's journey northward he would receive twice the wealth he now had; in the northern valley of Elankunnu lay an idol made of pathala-anjanam, the black stone of the underworld; and he should take that idol, invoke the Devi upon it, and have the installation performed.
p08s03: When he woke and opened his eyes, he saw no one anywhere.
p08s04: Still, he believed it was the Devi herself who had spoken to him.
p08s05: Setting out the next morning, the Bhattathiri went to the valley of Elankunnu — and there, just as in the dream, he found a Devi idol made of pathala-anjanam.
p08s06: At that his faith grew firmer still.
p08s07: At once he had the idol taken up and brought, had it laid in water in the rite of jalaadhivasam, had the temple built exactly as seen in the dream, had the installation and the kalasam performed at an auspicious moment, relinquished all his property to the Devi, entrusted the Devaswom's management to the Kochi Raja — and then went north.
p08s08: One evening he arrived at an illam, and having bathed, done his twilight worship and taken supper, he stayed there.
p08s09: At that time the householder of that illam — despondent from childlessness and enfeebled by old age — had been thinking of adopting people from a worthy illam and making them his heirs.
p08s10: After conversing a while with the Bhattathiri, it occurred to the householder that he might adopt this very man; he said as much; the Bhattathiri, remembering the dream he had had, consented fully — and before long the Bhattathiri and his family were adopted into that illam.
p08s11: That illam had more than twice the wealth the Bhattathiri's own had had.
p08s12: If it is added that this illam is the famous "Poomulli Mana" itself, everyone will know that its abundance has suffered no decline even now.
p08s13: It hardly needs saying that everyone at Poomulli Mana today belongs to the Thiruvalayannur Bhattathiri's line of descent.
p08s14: Even now the rule at the Ammathiruvadi temple is that no one may enter and perform puja inside except the Poomulli Namboothiripad, the Thantri, and the Melsanthi.
p08s15: Since it was the Thiruvalayannur Bhattathiri who served the Devi and brought her here, and who made his own illam her temple and had the installation performed, it needs no special telling that this is how the Urakam Ammathiruvadi came by the sacred name "Thiruvalayannur Bhagavathi".
p08s16: The offering made to the Devi each day immediately after the abhishekam is still provided at Poomulli Mana's expense.
p09s01: The spot within the temple walls — toward the west on the eastern side, where a jack tree with a granite platform now stands beside the circumambulation path — was in former times the courtyard of the Thiruvalayannur Bhattathiri's illam.
p09s02: It was on that spot that the Bhattathiri set down his palm-leaf umbrella on returning from Kanchipuram.
p09s03: That spot is now called the Sreemoolasthanam — the original sacred seat.
p09s04: At the Sreemoolasthanam too, regular puja and the people's worship continue to this day.
p09s05: If any impurity befalls that spot for any reason, a purification is required for the idol inside the sanctum as well.
p09s06: Because the Devi journeyed from Kanchipuram to Kerala dwelling upon a palm-leaf umbrella, the palm-leaf umbrella is still of first importance to the Ammathiruvadi.
p09s07: At all three daily seeveli processions it is still the palm-leaf umbrella that is held.
p09s08: Even the silk umbrella held on the elephant on days when she is taken out for the Pooram is made there with a palm-leaf umbrella joined inside it.
p10s01: The Ammathiruvadi temple remains a great temple to this day.
p10s02: The five daily pujas, three seevelis, navakam, panchagavyam and all such rites are still performed here regularly.
p11s01: Since the Devi is Karthyayani — a virgin — not only may fragrant flowers not be used here for puja, but gunfire, fireworks and other uses of blasting powder are not customary at the Pooram and other festivals.
p12s01: Within the sacred precinct of the Ammathiruvadi's temple, castes below the Nairs may not settle, nor may liquor, meat and the like be bought or sold.
p12s02: Even the Nairs and such castes have no right to roof their houses with tiles, to build a gatehouse joining all four corners, or to put up pavilions for their ceremonies.
p12s03: For tiling a house, for bringing jasmine on an elephant for a wedding, or for holding a feast with large pappadam, sugar and the like, one needs — along with the Devaswom's permission — a written order of the Valiya Thampuran himself.
p12s04: To obtain the Devaswom's permission to tile a house, one must lay a hundred and one panams at the Devi's shrine-door (a panam being four annas and seven pies).
p13s01: The Bhagavathis brought by Keezhottukara Namboothiripad and Komarathu Menon were likewise installed by them, each at his own place.
p13s02: The place where the Namboothiripad installed his Bhagavathi is called Pisharikkal, and where the Menon installed his, "Palisseri".
p13s03: All three — the Bhattathiri, the Namboothiripad and the Menon — had served and brought one and the same Bhagavathi.
p13s04: Yet — whether from a difference of meditation at the moment of installation, or whatever it was — the Menon's Bhagavathi turned out to be Bhadrakali.
p13s05: There the puja is conducted to this day as for Bhadrakali.
p13s06: It is still the custom that when that Bhagavathi is taken out in procession, the Komarathu Menon attends her as escort, sword in hand.
p14s01: Every year on the new-moon day of the month of Thulam, the Ammathiruvadi is taken out in procession, with the ceremonial bath in the Mampilly pond on the eastern side.
p14s02: Only on that day is she carried out through the eastern gopuram.
p14s03: On the other days when she is taken out, it is through the western gopuram.
p14s04: The reason given for this is as follows.
p15s01: Once, long ago, the Kodungallur Bhagavathi, touring the land for the protection of the world, arrived here one day and met the Ammathiruvadi; a friendship formed between the two; the Ammathiruvadi asked her to stay close by, and permitted her to sit atop the eastern gopuram — and accordingly, from that day, the Kodungallur Bhagavathi took up residence on top of the eastern gopuram.
p15s02: So if the Ammathiruvadi were carried out that way, she would be passing beneath the Kodungallur Bhagavathi's feet.
p15s03: That is why the procession by that route was given up.
p15s04: On the new-moon day of Thulam, the Kodungallur Bhagavathi leaves the previous night itself, to bathe in the sea.
p15s05: She returns only after the sea-bath and the rest are finished on the new-moon day.
p15s06: And while the Kodungallur Bhagavathi is away, there can be no objection to carrying the Ammathiruvadi that way.
p15s07: That is why it is held that the procession may go that way on that one day alone.
p15s08: Even now, people who come to this temple for darshanam customarily also worship toward the top of the eastern gopuram, meditating on the Kodungallur Bhagavathi.
p16s01: Something must now be said, briefly, about the origin of the Mampilly pond where the Bhagavathi has her ceremonial bath.
p16s02: Mampilly is the house-name of a Namboothiri's illam.
p16s03: It is said that this pond, as large as a great reservoir, was dug out in a single day.
p17s01: Once upon a time, male heirs became scarce in the Kochi royal family.
p17s02: So a princess of that time who was with child took up residence in prayer at the Urakam Ammathiruvadi temple, praying that the child she bore might be a son.
p17s03: During that vigil, one day, as the princess stepped down into the temple pond to wash her face and feet, a young brahmachari boy of the Mampilly illam was standing at the bathing ghat.
p17s04: That mischievous boy splashed muddy water onto the princess's upper cloth.
p17s05: Though the princess felt more than ordinary anger toward the boy, she climbed out without a word, kept the cloth with its mud unwashed, preserved exactly as it was; and when her term of prayer was complete she returned to the palace — and in due time gave birth to a son.
p17s06: That fortunate prince received royal authority in his eighteenth year itself.
p17s07: On the first birthday festival after he assumed the kingship, the Queen Mother — wearing that very mud-stained cloth she had preserved, just as it was — served the Thampuran his rice!
p17s08: Seeing the cloth, the Thampuran asked how it came to be so, and she told him the whole story.
p17s09: As soon as the royal meal was over and he came out, the Thampuran summoned the Diwanji before him: "Tomorrow morning my bath shall be at the place where the Mampilly Namboothiri's illam stands."
p17s10: "Let arrangements be made for it as required," he commanded.
p17s11: As commanded, the Diwanji that very day gathered the men needed, had the Namboothiri's illam demolished and removed, had a pond as large as a great reservoir dug where it had stood, had it purified before daybreak — and in the morning the Thampuran came and performed his ceremonial bath there.
p17s12: Such is the origin of the Mampilly pond.
p18s01: At the Ammathiruvadi temple, every year from the first of Vrischikam there is the forty-one-day vaaram, and on Karthika day the festival of lamps.
p18s02: The expenses of the Karthika-day feast and the rest used to be met by the Kochi government.
p19s01: The famous Arattupuzha Pooram falls in the month of Meenam.
p19s02: In connection with it, the purificatory kalasam and the rest are performed on Rohini day, and on Makayiram day the flag is raised.
p19s03: The principal ceremonial bath is on the eighth day, Uthram.
p19s04: It takes place each day at a different spot.
p19s05: Of these, the bath on the Makayiram and Thiruvathira days is customarily in the Mampilly pond.
p20s01: For the Arattupuzha Pooram, processions come from forty-one temples.
p20s02: In all those temples the custom is the flag-raising on Makayiram and the bath on Uthram.
p20s03: When they have all processed to Arattupuzha on Pooram day, it is only after the bath on Uthram morning that all the deities are carried back.
p20s04: The bath of all the deities is at the river-ghat of Arattupuzha itself.
p20s05: This must indeed be how the place came by the name "Arattupuzha" — the river of the sacred bath.
p20s06: Though processions come from forty-one temples, the primacy among them belongs to the Ammathiruvadi herself.
p20s07: For all the deities who process here, everything needed for the offerings after the Uthram-morning bath at the river-ghat is given by the Ammathiruvadi, from her Devaswom.
p20s08: But the Thripprayar deity — Sri Rama Swami — by reason of his lordship, and the Cherpu Bhagavathi, by reason of rivalry, alone accept none of it.
p20s09: The cause of the rivalry between the Ammathiruvadi and the Cherpu Bhagavathi will be told presently.
p21s01: In former times the Namboothiris of the Perumanam gramam, out of mutual rivalry, were in the habit of quarrelling almost constantly.
p21s02: It is because of their rivalry that the festival at the Perumanam temple itself ceased to be.
p21s03: There used to be a festival there once.
p21s04: There too it was conducted with the flag-raising on Makayiram day and the bath on Uthram day.
p21s05: Owing to the rivalry, quarrels and riots among the Namboothiris, it came to a stop.
p21s06: Then for some time there was not merely no festival there, but no celebration whatsoever.
p21s07: Even the regular pujas ceased to be performed properly.
p21s08: After things had gone on so, more than half the yogam members came together and decided: even without the festival, a celebration should be held once a year; since the Pooram at Arattupuzha was the principal one, Pooyam day would do for it; the Perumanathappan himself need not be taken out; on that day all the other deities of the gramam would be brought here in procession, made as splendid as means allowed — and so it was put into practice.
p21s09: That is the present Perumanam Pooram.
p22s01: Because the yogam members' rivalry sometimes threatened to interrupt the daily noon puja at the Perumanam temple and the annual Koodiyattam, the members who stood together made the Paravur Raja responsible for keeping them unbroken, and for that purpose a good many lands and house-plots were made over to that Raja from the Devaswom.
p22s02: When the Paravur territory was absorbed into Travancore, those properties too passed under the Travancore government's control.
p22s03: That is why the noon puja, Koodiyattam and the rest at the Perumanam temple — which lies in Kochi territory — are still maintained by the Travancore government.
p23s01: Even after all this, the rivalry and quarrelling of the yogam members did not entirely subside.
p23s02: The last great quarrel arose between the chief and most powerful men of the Perumanam gramam — the Avanaa Mana Namboothiripad and the Chittur Mana Namboothiripad.
p23s03: After the Perumanam temple's affairs had been settled after a fashion, as told above, the Chittur Namboothiripad turned against the Ammathiruvadi.
p23s04: His aim was to reduce that temple, by any means, to a mere name.
p23s05: To that end he tried many devices.
p23s06: Since the Avanaa Mana Namboothiripad kept blocking them all with sharp vigilance, no great harm befell the Ammathiruvadi Devaswom.
p23s07: The Avanaa Mana Namboothiripad had the Ammathiruvadi's favour and the help of good people in abundance.
p23s08: For the Chittur Namboothiripad, both were very scarce.
p23s09: That is why his design did not succeed.
p24s01: The Chittur Namboothiripad had secretly laid certain plans to cause mishaps while the Ammathiruvadi was being taken in procession to the Perumanam Pooram.
p24s02: The Avanaa Mana Namboothiripad, learning of this through his spies, arranged that when the Ammathiruvadi went to Perumanam, his own Sastha of Chathamkudam should go in procession along with her, with many escorts; and he sent instruction that when she was carried inside the Perumanam walls for the circumambulation, the Sastha should go in front.
p24s03: So that design of the Chittur Namboothiripad also came to nothing.
p24s04: Even now, though the Devaswom Board provides no escorts, when the Ammathiruvadi goes to the Perumanam Pooram, the Avanaa Mana Namboothiripad still sends his Chathamkudam Sastha in procession along with her.
p24s05: The custom is still that the Sastha goes in front when entering the walls, and the Bhagavathi in front on the way back.
p24s06: Though the Avanaa Mana Namboothiripad always took every precaution that no harm should befall the Ammathiruvadi, one small loss could not be avoided.
p24s07: Once, at the Perumanam Pooram, as the Ammathiruvadi was being carried in through the southern gopuram, some men sitting on top of it hooked away the golden finial of her umbrella and made off with it.
p24s08: This, surely, was the work of the Chittur Namboothiripad's agents.
p24s09: At any rate, from that time the Ammathiruvadi is never taken into the Perumanam walls through that gopuram.
p25s01: None of this satisfied the Chittur Namboothiripad.
p25s02: At last he won over the Padinjaredathu Bhattathiri, the Thantri of the Ammathiruvadi temple, and arranged with him to invoke the Devi with flower and water into a conch, carry it to Chittur Mana, and hand it over.
p25s03: When the Thantri, having so invoked the Devi, had gone as far as the north-east corner outside the wall, the conch fell from his hand to the ground — and from it a radiance too dazzling to look upon was seen to rise and pass into the temple.
p25s04: From that day, all who come for darshanam took to touching the wall at the spot where the conch fell, and laying that hand upon their heads.
p25s06: The yogam members, taking counsel together, removed that very day the Thantri who had practised treachery at the Chittur Namboothiripad's word, and appointed another to the office.
p25s07: By now the Chittur Namboothiripad was somewhat subdued; convinced at last that his design would never succeed, he built a temple of his own, had a Bhagavathi installed there too, and began conducting Pooram and other celebrations.
p25s09: Matters standing so, it is no wonder that that Bhagavathi bears rivalry toward the Ammathiruvadi.
p25s10: But since rivalry can arise between human beings, and not between Devis, this can only be a human imagining.
p25s11: It is partisan men who, in the spirit of rivalry, still strive to make the Cherpu Bhagavathi's procession at the Arattupuzha Pooram more ornate and splendid than the Ammathiruvadi's.
p25s12: It has been said that processions from forty-one temples come to the Arattupuzha Pooram.
p25s13: Of these, the processions of the Thripprayar Sri Rama Swami, the Urakam Ammathiruvadi and the Cherpu Bhagavathi are still the grandest and most splendidly conducted.
p25s14: The Ammathiruvadi's procession customarily has twenty-nine elephants.
p25s15: The Cherpu Bhagavathi's procession never has fewer.
p25s16: In some years there have been more.
p25s17: With upwards of seventy first-rate elephants — most of them tuskers — with countless torches in four rows before them, the Thripprayar deity in the centre, the Urakam Ammathiruvadi on his left and the Cherpu Bhagavathi on his right, that procession across the Arattupuzha fields is truly a festival for the beholder's eyes.
p25s18: To take in the full beauty of that procession, one must watch from a little distance.
p25s19: Majestic with its multitude of elephants and the thunder of the melam, and serenely beautiful in its freedom from fireworks — whoever sees that procession will surely feel that the saying that even the gods come to watch it is no mere exaggeration.
p25s20: And the chief cause of that, too, is the Ammathiruvadi's own greatness.
p26s01: In former times a maiden of Keezhottukara Mana used to go daily to the Ammathiruvadi's temple for darshanam.
p26s02: After some time had passed so, the maiden came to the threshold of youth.
p26s03: Then her father the Namboothiripad resolved to give her in marriage to a Namboothiri who lived far away; the auspicious hour was fixed, everything was prepared, and all who were needed were invited.
p26s04: On the day before the wedding, the Namboothiri chosen as bridegroom, those needed for the rites, the Othikkon and the rest all arrived.
p26s05: The auspicious hour fell at night.
p26s06: That morning the maiden bathed as usual, came to the Ammathiruvadi temple, and standing before the shrine for darshanam: "My Devi, when will I ever come here again for your darshanam?
p26s07: I doubt it will ever again be possible for me.
p26s08: It seems to me better to die than to live without coming here for darshanam even one day.
p26s09: Therefore, Devi, mercifully grant that I may die this very day" — so she prayed, weeping, hands folded.
p26s10: Then the Devi: "Do not grieve at all.
p26s11: Come to me" — so she seemed to the maiden to say, stretching her sacred hands toward her.
p26s12: At once the maiden ran into the sanctum.
p26s13: She clasped the Devi's idol tightly — and there she merged into oneness with her.
p26s14: From that time, the women and maidens of Keezhottukara Mana ceased to come to the Ammathiruvadi temple for darshanam.
p26s15: After some time, the Namboothiripad petitioned the Devaswom that the Ammathiruvadi be brought in procession to the Mana once a year, so that the secluded women of the house might have darshanam; the Devaswom agreed that after the Pooram flag-raising she could come to the Mana for one day before the bath; and so the processions began.
p26s16: Every year, after the flag is raised, on one day before the bath, the Ammathiruvadi is still taken in procession to Keezhottukara Mana, with the setting-down puja and the rest.
p26s17: When she has been carried in and the Thantri's puja is done, the processional image is set in the eastern hall, and the Thantri withdraws.
p26s18: Then the women of the house come and perform their pujas, offerings and worship — and only after that is she carried back.
p26s19: It is their belief that the offerings of the antharjanams please the Devi more than the Thantri's own.
p27s01: At a seeveli procession the custom, as everyone knows, is that the deity follows behind the priest, who goes ahead sprinkling the offerings on the bali-stones.
p27s02: But at the Ammathiruvadi temple the custom for the night seeveli is that the Devi is carried in front, and the priest walks behind her, sprinkling.
p27s03: It became so here at some point along the way.
p27s04: The reason that has been heard for it is given below:
p28s01: Long ago a Gandharva used to come daily for darshanam — at Thiruvanchikulam for the deeparadhana, at the Urakam temple for the night seeveli, and at the Thrissivaperur Vadakkunnatha temple for the thrippuka.
p28s02: One day, by the time that Gandharva had worshipped at Thiruvanchikulam and reached Urakam, he found the deity already carried out for the night seeveli.
p28s03: Fearing the seeveli might end and the shrine-door close before he could finish his customary circumambulation and prostrations, the Gandharva stopped the Melsanthi — who was walking ahead, sprinkling — and frightened him.
p28s04: At that the priest fell senseless and stood transfixed for a while.
p28s05: Only after the Devi had been carried up beside him did he come back to his senses.
p28s06: With the Devi near, no Gandharva or anyone else can frighten a person, whatever he may attempt.
p28s07: So it was decided from that time that at the night seeveli, the Melsanthi should begin his sprinkling only after the Ammathiruvadi had been carried out and stationed on the north side of the flagstaff platform.
p28s08: And so it is still done to this day.
p29s01: From this legend it is plain that even beings of divine birth come regularly to worship the Ammathiruvadi.
p29s02: Though that now lies beyond our sight, it is a matter of everyone's living experience that many people still come and worship the Devi here — for children, for wealth, for learning and much else — and that all obtain their heart's desire.
p30s01: "Ever granting to the throngs who serve her all that they conceive, like a wish-granting creeper to this world, renowned upon the earth, the Devi dwells in the Valaya abode brimming with splendour — whoever presses her feet into the flower of the heart and bows shall straightway find good fortune arise."
